question_text: The modern periodic law states that the properties of elements are a periodic function of their:
explanation: The modern periodic law was proposed by Henry Moseley in 1913. It states that the physical and chemical properties of elements are a periodic function of their atomic numbers (Z). This replaced Mendeleev's periodic law which was based on atomic masses. Moseley discovered that the atomic number (number of protons) is the fundamental property that determines the chemical behaviour of an element, not atomic mass.
topic: Modern Periodic Table and s,p,d,f Blocks
subject: chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties

options.text: Number of electrons in the outermost shell

elimination_hints.hint: Atomic mass was the basis of Mendeleev's original periodic law, not the modern periodic law. Isotopes of the same element have different atomic masses but identical chemical properties.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing Mendeleev's periodic law (based on atomic mass) with the modern periodic law (based on atomic number).

elimination_hints.hint: Number of neutrons varies among isotopes of the same element and does not determine chemical periodicity.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing neutrons with protons as the defining nuclear property for periodic classification.

elimination_hints.hint: While valence electrons determine chemical behaviour, the periodic law is stated in terms of atomic number, not just the outermost electrons.
elimination_hints.misconception: Mixing up the cause of periodicity (electron configuration) with the statement of the periodic law (atomic number).

question_text: The total number of periods in the modern periodic table (long form) is:
explanation: The long form of the modern periodic table contains 7 periods. Period 1 has 2 elements (H, He), Period 2 has 8 elements, Period 3 has 8 elements, Period 4 has 18 elements, Period 5 has 18 elements, Period 6 has 32 elements, and Period 7 is incomplete. Each period starts with the filling of a new principal energy level (shell).
topic: Modern Periodic Table and s,p,d,f Blocks
subject: chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties

elimination_hints.hint: 4 periods would only accommodate elements up to atomic number 36 (Krypton). The periodic table has many more elements beyond that.
elimination_hints.misconception: Significantly underestimating the number of periods in the periodic table.

elimination_hints.hint: 6 periods would leave out all the elements in the 7th period, including the actinides and recently discovered superheavy elements.
elimination_hints.misconception: Forgetting the 7th period which contains actinides and transactinide elements.

elimination_hints.hint: 9 periods do not exist in the current periodic table. The table only extends up to 7 periods based on known elements.
elimination_hints.misconception: Overestimating the number of periods; possibly confusing with total number of groups in s-block.

question_text: Which block of the periodic table contains the alkali and alkaline earth metals?
explanation: The s-block of the periodic table contains elements in which the last electron enters the s-orbital. Group 1 elements (alkali metals: Li, Na, K, Rb, Cs, Fr) have the general electronic configuration ns¹, and Group 2 elements (alkaline earth metals: Be, Mg, Ca, Sr, Ba, Ra) have the configuration ns². Hydrogen and helium also belong to the s-block. These elements are typically highly reactive metals (except H and He) and strong reducing agents.
topic: Modern Periodic Table and s,p,d,f Blocks
subject: chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties

elimination_hints.hint: The p-block contains groups 13–18. It includes metalloids, non-metals, and some metals, but not the alkali or alkaline earth metals.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing s-block with p-block; alkali metals are in group 1 (s-block), not in the p-block.

elimination_hints.hint: The d-block contains transition metals (groups 3–12). These are elements where the last electron enters a d-orbital, not an s-orbital.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing s-block metals with d-block transition metals.

elimination_hints.hint: The f-block contains the lanthanides and actinides, which are placed separately at the bottom of the periodic table.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing s-block with f-block inner transition elements.

question_text: The total number of groups in the modern periodic table is:
explanation: The long form of the modern periodic table has 18 groups (vertical columns). Groups 1 and 2 belong to the s-block, groups 3–12 belong to the d-block, and groups 13–18 belong to the p-block. The f-block elements (lanthanides and actinides) are placed separately at the bottom and are not assigned specific group numbers. The IUPAC numbering system uses 1–18 to designate all groups.
topic: Modern Periodic Table and s,p,d,f Blocks
subject: chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties

elimination_hints.hint: 7 is the number of periods in the periodic table, not the number of groups.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the number of periods (7) with the number of groups (18).

elimination_hints.hint: 8 groups existed in the old Mendeleev periodic table (groups I–VIII), not in the modern long form periodic table.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the old Mendeleev group numbering system with the modern IUPAC system.

elimination_hints.hint: 16 does not correspond to any standard count of groups. The modern periodic table has 18 vertical columns.
elimination_hints.misconception: Possibly confusing with the number of groups in p-block and d-block combined minus 2.

question_text: An element has the electronic configuration [Ar] 3d⁶ 4s². The element belongs to:
explanation: The electronic configuration [Ar] 3d⁶ 4s² indicates that the element has argon core and additional electrons filling the 3d and 4s orbitals. Since the last electron enters the 3d orbital, this element belongs to the d-block. It is in the 4th period (highest principal quantum number n = 4) and belongs to group 8 (total d + s electrons = 6 + 2 = 8). This is the configuration of iron (Fe, Z = 26), which is a d-block transition element.
topic: Modern Periodic Table and s,p,d,f Blocks
subject: chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties

elimination_hints.hint: While the element is in the 4th period, the last electron enters a d-orbital (3d⁶), not an s-orbital. The s-block only includes groups 1 and 2.
elimination_hints.misconception: Assuming that because 4s is filled, the element is in the s-block. Block is determined by the last orbital being filled.

elimination_hints.hint: The highest principal quantum number is 4 (from 4s²), so the element is in the 4th period, not the 3rd. Don't confuse 3d with 3rd period.
elimination_hints.misconception: Using the d-orbital principal quantum number (3d) to determine the period instead of the highest n value.

elimination_hints.hint: There are no p-electrons beyond the Ar core in this configuration. The differentiating electron enters a d-orbital, making it a d-block element.
elimination_hints.misconception: Incorrectly identifying the block; no p-orbital filling is indicated after the noble gas core.

question_text: An element with atomic number 35 belongs to which group and period in the modern periodic table?
explanation: Element with Z = 35 is Bromine (Br). Its electronic configuration is [Ar] 3d¹⁰ 4s² 4p⁵. The highest principal quantum number is 4, so it is in the 4th period. The last electron enters the 4p orbital, making it a p-block element. For p-block elements, group number = 10 + (number of electrons in ns + np orbitals) = 10 + 2 + 5 = 17. So bromine is in Group 17 (halogens), Period 4.
topic: Modern Periodic Table and s,p,d,f Blocks
subject: chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties

elimination_hints.hint: Group 15 elements have the valence configuration ns²np³. Bromine has ns²np⁵, which places it in group 17, not 15.
elimination_hints.misconception: Miscounting the number of valence electrons or confusing the group number formula for p-block elements.

elimination_hints.hint: Period 3 elements have their outermost electrons in the 3rd shell. Bromine has 4s and 4p electrons, placing it in the 4th period.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing bromine (Period 4) with chlorine (Period 3), both of which are halogens.

elimination_hints.hint: Group 7 is a d-block group (containing Mn, Tc, Re). Halogens are in Group 17 in the IUPAC numbering, not Group 7.
elimination_hints.misconception: Using the old group numbering (VII-A or VII-B) instead of the modern IUPAC numbering (1–18).

question_text: The IUPAC name of the element with atomic number 109 is:
explanation: For elements with atomic number greater than 100, IUPAC uses a systematic nomenclature based on Latin/Greek numerical roots. The digits of 109 are: 1 → un, 0 → nil, 9 → enn. So the name is Unnilennium (Une). However, element 109 has since been officially named Meitnerium (Mt). The IUPAC systematic name before official naming was Unnilennium. For NEET purposes, the IUPAC nomenclature system for unnamed elements uses roots: 0=nil, 1=un, 2=bi, 3=tri, 4=quad, 5=pent, 6=hex, 7=sept, 8=oct, 9=enn, followed by the suffix '-ium'.
topic: Modern Periodic Table and s,p,d,f Blocks
subject: chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties

elimination_hints.hint: This is not a valid IUPAC systematic name. The digits of 109 are 1-0-9, which correspond to un-nil-enn, not un-nil-oct.
elimination_hints.misconception: Incorrectly applying the numerical roots; confusing 9 (enn) with 8 (oct).

elimination_hints.hint: Unnilseptium would correspond to atomic number 107 (1-0-7). The element asked is 109, not 107.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the digit 9 (enn) with 7 (sept) when constructing the IUPAC name.

elimination_hints.hint: Ununennium (Uue) would correspond to atomic number 119 (1-1-9), not 109 (1-0-9).
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing 0 (nil) with 1 (un) in the second position, leading to an incorrect name.

question_text: The lanthanides and actinides are placed at the bottom of the periodic table because they belong to the:
explanation: Lanthanides (Ce to Lu, Z = 58–71) and actinides (Th to Lr, Z = 90–103) are f-block elements. In these elements, the differentiating electron enters the 4f orbital (lanthanides) or 5f orbital (actinides). They are called inner transition elements because the f-orbitals being filled are in the (n−2) shell, which is two shells inside the outermost shell. They are placed separately at the bottom of the periodic table to maintain the table's manageable width.
topic: Modern Periodic Table and s,p,d,f Blocks
subject: chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties

elimination_hints.hint: The s-block contains groups 1 and 2 (alkali and alkaline earth metals). Lanthanides and actinides involve f-orbital filling, not s-orbital.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing s-block with f-block; s-block elements do not include lanthanides or actinides.

elimination_hints.hint: The p-block contains groups 13–18 with the last electron entering p-orbitals. Lanthanides and actinides have f-orbital filling.
elimination_hints.misconception: Mixing up p-block representative elements with inner transition f-block elements.

elimination_hints.hint: While lanthanides and actinides are sometimes loosely associated with d-block (they technically sit within Period 6 and 7 in the d-block region), they are specifically classified as f-block because their differentiating electron enters f-orbitals.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing d-block transition elements with f-block inner transition elements.

question_text: Which of the following elements is a metalloid?
explanation: Silicon (Si, Z = 14) is a metalloid (semi-metal). Metalloids have properties intermediate between metals and non-metals. They are found along the zig-zag line (staircase) in the periodic table separating metals from non-metals. The commonly recognised metalloids are: Boron (B), Silicon (Si), Germanium (Ge), Arsenic (As), Antimony (Sb), Tellurium (Te), and Polonium (Po). Sodium is a metal (s-block), chlorine and argon are non-metals (p-block).
topic: Modern Periodic Table and s,p,d,f Blocks
subject: chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties

elimination_hints.hint: Sodium is a Group 1 alkali metal — a highly reactive metal that vigorously reacts with water. It is not a metalloid.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing a reactive s-block metal with a metalloid.

elimination_hints.hint: Chlorine is a Group 17 halogen — a non-metal and a strong oxidizing agent. It does not have intermediate properties.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing a reactive non-metal (halogen) with a metalloid.

elimination_hints.hint: Argon is a Group 18 noble gas — completely non-metallic and chemically inert. Noble gases are not metalloids.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing an inert noble gas with a metalloid.

question_text: The 3rd period of the periodic table contains 8 elements because:
explanation: In the 3rd period, the electrons fill the 3s and 3p orbitals. The 3s orbital can hold a maximum of 2 electrons and the 3p orbitals can hold a maximum of 6 electrons, giving a total of 2 + 6 = 8 electrons. Since each new element adds one electron, there are 8 elements in the 3rd period (Na to Ar). The 3d orbitals, although part of the 3rd shell, have higher energy than the 4s orbital and are filled in the 4th period, not the 3rd.
topic: Modern Periodic Table and s,p,d,f Blocks
subject: chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties

options.text: Only 3s orbital is filled in this period
3s and 3p orbitals are filled, accommodating 2 + 6 = 8 electrons
3s, 3p, and 3d orbitals are filled, accommodating 18 electrons
Only 3p orbitals are filled in this period

elimination_hints.hint: If only 3s were filled, the period would have only 2 elements. The 3rd period has 8 elements, so more orbitals must be involved.
elimination_hints.misconception: Forgetting that both 3s and 3p orbitals are filled during the 3rd period.

elimination_hints.hint: The 3d orbitals are NOT filled in the 3rd period. Due to the Aufbau principle, 4s fills before 3d, so 3d filling occurs in the 4th period, resulting in 18 elements there.
elimination_hints.misconception: Ignoring the Aufbau principle and assuming all orbitals of the 3rd shell fill in the 3rd period.

elimination_hints.hint: If only 3p were filled, only 6 elements would be in this period. Both 3s (2 electrons) and 3p (6 electrons) are filled.
elimination_hints.misconception: Forgetting the 3s orbital contribution to the 3rd period.

question_text: The electronic configuration of Cr (Z = 24) is [Ar] 3d⁵ 4s¹ instead of [Ar] 3d⁴ 4s². This is because:
explanation: Chromium (Z = 24) has the electronic configuration [Ar] 3d⁵ 4s¹ instead of the expected [Ar] 3d⁴ 4s². This anomaly occurs because a half-filled d-subshell (3d⁵) provides extra stability due to the symmetrical distribution of electrons and maximum exchange energy. The exchange energy is the stabilization energy resulting from electrons with parallel spins exchanging positions. With 3d⁵ 4s¹, all six electrons have parallel spins across the 3d and 4s orbitals, maximizing exchange energy. This extra stabilization outweighs the energy gained from filling the 4s orbital completely.
topic: Modern Periodic Table and s,p,d,f Blocks
subject: chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties

options.text: Half-filled d-subshell (d⁵) has extra stability due to symmetry and exchange energy
The 4s orbital has higher energy than 3d for all elements
Pairing energy of 4s electrons is always too high
Chromium cannot have electrons in the 4s orbital

elimination_hints.hint: The relative energies of 4s and 3d depend on the atomic number and the nuclear charge. This is not a universal rule; the anomaly in Cr is specifically due to the extra stability of a half-filled configuration.
elimination_hints.misconception: Overgeneralizing the energy ordering of orbitals without considering the specific stability factors.

elimination_hints.hint: While pairing energy plays a role, the specific reason for Cr's anomalous configuration is the extra stability of the half-filled d⁵ arrangement, not merely a high pairing energy.
elimination_hints.misconception: Attributing the anomaly solely to pairing energy rather than the combined effect of exchange energy and half-filled stability.

elimination_hints.hint: Chromium does have an electron in the 4s orbital (4s¹). The configuration is [Ar] 3d⁵ 4s¹, which shows one 4s electron.
elimination_hints.misconception: Incorrectly assuming Cr has no 4s electrons at all.

question_text: An element X has the electronic configuration [Xe] 4f¹⁴ 5d¹ 6s². Element X belongs to which group and which block?
explanation: The electronic configuration [Xe] 4f¹⁴ 5d¹ 6s² shows that the 4f subshell is completely filled (14 electrons) and the differentiating (last) electron enters the 5d orbital. Since the last electron enters a d-orbital, the element belongs to the d-block. For d-block elements, the group number = number of (n−1)d electrons + ns electrons. Here, d-electrons = 1 and s-electrons = 2, so group = 1 + 2 = 3. The element is in Group 3, Period 6 (since the highest principal quantum number is 6). This is the element Lutetium (Lu, Z = 71). Note: Although Lu has a filled 4f subshell, IUPAC classifies it as a d-block element (Group 3) because its differentiating electron is in 5d.
topic: Modern Periodic Table and s,p,d,f Blocks
subject: chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties

elimination_hints.hint: Group 2 elements have ns² as their valence configuration with no d-electrons. Here the element has 5d¹ electrons, so it cannot be in Group 2 or the s-block.
elimination_hints.misconception: Focusing only on the 6s² electrons and ignoring the 5d¹ electron which determines the block.

elimination_hints.hint: Although the element has 14 f-electrons, the 4f subshell is completely filled. The differentiating electron is in 5d, making it a d-block element, not f-block.
elimination_hints.misconception: Assuming that presence of f-electrons automatically makes an element an f-block element, regardless of which is the differentiating electron.

elimination_hints.hint: Group 1 would require only ns¹ configuration. This element has 5d¹ 6s², giving a total of 3 electrons in the outermost d and s orbitals, corresponding to Group 3.
elimination_hints.misconception: Counting only the d-electrons for the group number instead of adding d + s electrons.

question_text:
Assertion (A): Elements in the same group of the periodic table have similar chemical properties.

Reason (R): Elements in the same group have the same number of valence electrons and similar outermost electronic configurations.
explanation: Both the assertion and the reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion. Elements in the same group have similar valence electron configurations (e.g., all Group 1 elements have ns¹, all Group 17 elements have ns²np⁵). Since chemical properties are primarily determined by the number and arrangement of valence electrons, elements with the same valence configuration exhibit similar chemical behaviour. For example, all alkali metals (Group 1) are highly reactive, form +1 ions, and react vigorously with water.
topic: Modern Periodic Table and s,p,d,f Blocks
subject: chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties

options.text: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true

elimination_hints.hint: The reason directly explains the assertion. Valence electron configuration is the actual cause of similar chemical properties within a group, making R the correct explanation.
elimination_hints.misconception: Failing to connect valence electron configuration as the direct cause of chemical similarity within a group.

elimination_hints.hint: The reason is factually true. Elements in the same group do have the same number of valence electrons and similar outermost configurations (e.g., all Group 1 elements have ns¹).
elimination_hints.misconception: Incorrectly judging the statement about valence electrons as false.

elimination_hints.hint: The assertion is factually true. Elements in the same group are well known to have similar chemical properties — this is a fundamental principle of the periodic table.
elimination_hints.misconception: Denying the established fact that group members share similar chemical properties.

question_text:
Assertion (A): The d-block elements are called transition elements because they are situated between the s-block and p-block in the periodic table.

Reason (R): In d-block elements, the last electron enters the (n−1)d orbital, where n is the outermost shell.
explanation: The assertion is true — d-block elements (Groups 3–12) are positioned between the s-block (Groups 1–2) and p-block (Groups 13–18), and they exhibit a transition in properties from the highly electropositive s-block metals to the p-block elements. The reason is also true — in d-block elements, the differentiating electron enters the (n−1)d orbital. However, the reason does not correctly explain the assertion. The reason explains which orbital is being filled, but the assertion is about their physical position in the table and the transition in properties. The correct explanation for why they are called 'transition' elements is that they represent a transition in properties from s-block to p-block, which relates to their position, not merely to the orbital being filled.
topic: Modern Periodic Table and s,p,d,f Blocks
subject: chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties

options.text: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true

elimination_hints.hint: While both statements are true, the reason explains the orbital filling pattern of d-block elements, not why they are called 'transition' elements or why they are placed between s-block and p-block.
elimination_hints.misconception: Assuming that the orbital filling explanation automatically justifies the naming and positioning of transition elements.

elimination_hints.hint: The reason is factually correct. In d-block elements, the differentiating electron does enter the (n−1)d orbital. For example, in the 4th period d-block, electrons fill 3d orbitals.
elimination_hints.misconception: Incorrectly judging the statement about (n−1)d orbital filling as false.

elimination_hints.hint: The assertion is correct. D-block elements are indeed positioned between s-block and p-block and are called transition elements for this reason.
elimination_hints.misconception: Denying the well-established position of d-block elements between s-block and p-block.

question_text:
Match the following blocks of the periodic table with their corresponding orbital filling:

Column-I:
(P) s-block
(Q) p-block
(R) d-block
(S) f-block

Column-II:
(i) Filling of (n−2)f orbitals
(ii) Filling of ns orbitals
(iii) Filling of (n−1)d orbitals
(iv) Filling of np orbitals
explanation: s-block elements have their differentiating electron entering the ns orbital (P → ii). p-block elements have their last electron entering the np orbital (Q → iv). d-block elements have their last electron entering the (n−1)d orbital, where n is the outermost shell (R → iii). f-block elements have their last electron entering the (n−2)f orbital, which is two shells inside the valence shell (S → i). The correct matching is: P-ii, Q-iv, R-iii, S-i.
topic: Modern Periodic Table and s,p,d,f Blocks
subject: chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties

elimination_hints.hint: This option incorrectly matches p-block with (n−1)d orbital filling and d-block with np orbital filling. The p-block involves np orbital filling, not d-orbital.
elimination_hints.misconception: Swapping the orbital types for p-block and d-block elements.

elimination_hints.hint: This option incorrectly assigns (n−2)f filling to s-block and ns filling to f-block. The s-block fills ns orbitals, not f-orbitals.
elimination_hints.misconception: Swapping the s-block and f-block orbital assignments.

elimination_hints.hint: This option swaps d-block and f-block assignments. The d-block fills (n−1)d orbitals, not (n−2)f orbitals.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing d-block orbital filling with f-block orbital filling.

question_text:
Match the following elements with their correct position in the periodic table:

Column-I (Electronic Configuration):
(P) [He] 2s² 2p⁵
(Q) [Ar] 3d¹⁰ 4s¹
(R) [Xe] 4f⁷ 6s²
(S) [Kr] 5s²

Column-II (Position):
(i) Group 2, Period 5, s-block
(ii) Group 11, Period 4, d-block
(iii) Group 17, Period 2, p-block
(iv) Period 6, f-block
explanation:
(P) [He] 2s² 2p⁵: The highest principal quantum number is 2 (Period 2), and the last electron enters 2p (p-block). Valence electrons = 2 + 5 = 7, group = 10 + 7 = 17. This is Fluorine (F), Group 17, Period 2, p-block → (iii).
(Q) [Ar] 3d¹⁰ 4s¹: Highest n = 4 (Period 4), last electron enters 3d (d-block). Group = 10 + 1 = 11. This is Copper (Cu), Group 11, Period 4, d-block → (ii).
(R) [Xe] 4f⁷ 6s²: Highest n = 6 (Period 6), last electron enters 4f (f-block). This is Europium (Eu), Period 6, f-block → (iv).
(S) [Kr] 5s²: Highest n = 5 (Period 5), last electron enters 5s (s-block). Group 2. This is Strontium (Sr), Group 2, Period 5, s-block → (i).
Correct matching: P-iii, Q-ii, R-iv, S-i.
topic: Modern Periodic Table and s,p,d,f Blocks
subject: chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties

elimination_hints.hint: This option places Cu ([Ar] 3d¹⁰ 4s¹) as an f-block element, which is incorrect. Cu is a d-block element in Group 11.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing d-block copper with f-block elements; 3d¹⁰ 4s¹ clearly indicates d-block.

elimination_hints.hint: This option places F ([He] 2s² 2p⁵) in Group 11 d-block, which is impossible since fluorine has no d-electrons and is in Period 2.
elimination_hints.misconception: Swapping the positions of fluorine (p-block) and copper (d-block).

elimination_hints.hint: This option places Sr ([Kr] 5s²) as an f-block element, but Sr has no f-electrons. It is an s-block element in Group 2.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing s-block strontium with f-block elements; [Kr] 5s² has no f-orbital involvement.

question_text: True or False: Noble gases (Group 18) belong to the p-block of the periodic table, except helium which belongs to the s-block.
explanation: This statement is TRUE. Noble gases are in Group 18 (the last group of the p-block). Neon through Oganesson have their last electron entering the np orbital, making them p-block elements. However, helium (He, Z = 2) has the electronic configuration 1s², and its last electron enters the 1s orbital (an s-orbital). Therefore, helium belongs to the s-block, even though it is placed in Group 18 due to its chemical inertness and filled outer shell properties similar to other noble gases.
topic: Modern Periodic Table and s,p,d,f Blocks
subject: chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties

elimination_hints.hint: The statement is actually correct. Helium has configuration 1s² (s-block), while other noble gases (Ne, Ar, Kr, Xe, Rn, Og) have their last electron in p-orbitals (p-block).
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking all noble gases belong to the same block because they are in the same group.

elimination_hints.hint: This is a placeholder option and not a valid answer for this true-false question.
elimination_hints.misconception: N/A

elimination_hints.hint: This is a placeholder option and not a valid answer for this true-false question.
elimination_hints.misconception: N/A

question_text: True or False: The number of elements in the 4th period of the periodic table is 8, same as the 3rd period.
explanation: This statement is FALSE. The 3rd period has 8 elements (Na to Ar) because only 3s and 3p orbitals are filled. However, the 4th period has 18 elements (K to Kr) because 4s, 3d, and 4p orbitals are all filled in this period. The 4s orbital holds 2 electrons, the 3d orbitals hold 10 electrons, and the 4p orbitals hold 6 electrons, giving a total of 2 + 10 + 6 = 18 elements. The inclusion of d-orbital filling makes the 4th period longer than the 3rd.
topic: Modern Periodic Table and s,p,d,f Blocks
subject: chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties

elimination_hints.hint: The 4th period includes d-block elements (3d filling: Sc to Zn), making it contain 18 elements, not 8. The 3rd and 4th periods are not equal in length.
elimination_hints.misconception: Assuming all periods have the same number of elements, ignoring the introduction of d-orbital filling in the 4th period.

elimination_hints.hint: This is a placeholder option and not a valid answer for this true-false question.
elimination_hints.misconception: N/A

elimination_hints.hint: This is a placeholder option and not a valid answer for this true-false question.
elimination_hints.misconception: N/A

question_text: In the IUPAC nomenclature for elements with Z > 100, the element with atomic number 104 is named ________ with symbol ________.
explanation: Using IUPAC nomenclature for elements with atomic number greater than 100: the digits of 104 are 1, 0, and 4. The corresponding roots are: 1 → un, 0 → nil, 4 → quad. Adding the suffix '-ium' gives Unnilquadium. The symbol is derived from the first letter of each root: U (un), n (nil), q (quad) → Unq. So, the IUPAC systematic name is Unnilquadium (Unq). This element has been officially named Rutherfordium (Rf), but the IUPAC systematic nomenclature system is important for NEET.
topic: Modern Periodic Table and s,p,d,f Blocks
subject: chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties

elimination_hints.hint: Ununnilium would correspond to atomic number 110 (1-1-0). The element in question is 104, where the middle digit is 0 (nil), not 1 (un).
elimination_hints.misconception: Incorrectly converting the digits 1-0-4 to roots; confusing the second digit 0 with 1.

elimination_hints.hint: Unnilpentium would correspond to atomic number 105 (1-0-5). The last digit is 4 (quad), not 5 (pent).
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing quad (4) with pent (5) in the IUPAC numerical roots.

elimination_hints.hint: Ununquadium would correspond to atomic number 114 (1-1-4), not 104 (1-0-4). The middle digit is 0, not 1.
elimination_hints.misconception: Replacing nil (0) with un (1) in the second position.

question_text: A scientist discovers a new element X with atomic number 119. When writing the expected electronic configuration, it is found that the last electron enters a new energy level not previously occupied. Based on the position of this element in the periodic table, which of the following correctly describes element X?
explanation: Element 119 would have the electronic configuration [Og] 8s¹. Since oganesson (Og, Z = 118) completes the 7th period and the p-block (Group 18), element 119 would start a new period — the 8th period. The last electron enters the 8s orbital, making it an s-block element. With one electron in the outermost s-orbital, it would belong to Group 1 (alkali metal family). Its IUPAC systematic name would be Ununennium (Uue) from: 1→un, 1→un, 9→enn + ium. It is expected to be a highly reactive alkali metal, even more reactive than francium.
topic: Modern Periodic Table and s,p,d,f Blocks
subject: chemistry
chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties

elimination_hints.hint: Period 7 ends at Z = 118 (Oganesson). Element 119 starts a new period (Period 8). Also, Ununoctium corresponds to Z = 118, not 119.
elimination_hints.misconception: Failing to recognize that Z = 119 starts a new period and confusing the IUPAC names for 118 and 119.

elimination_hints.hint: Group 18 is the last group of the p-block, which ends a period. Element 119, being the first element of a new period, would start with s-orbital filling (Group 1), not end with p-orbital filling (Group 18).
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the start of a period (Group 1, s-block) with the end of a period (Group 18, p-block).

elimination_hints.hint: Group 2 elements have ns² configuration. Element 119 with configuration [Og] 8s¹ has only one s-electron, placing it in Group 1, not Group 2.
elimination_hints.misconception: Miscounting the number of valence electrons; 8s¹ indicates Group 1, not Group 2.
